text.title: pyproject.toml as the Single Source of Configuration Failure Modes
text.summary: Three antipatterns observed when consolidating Python config. Each pairs symptom + root-cause + concrete fix.

antipattern.symptom: New tool added; its config lands in a fresh `.toolnamerc` file beside pyproject.toml.
antipattern.root-cause: README example uses `.toolnamerc`; author missed that the tool also reads `[tool.<name>]`.
antipattern.fix: If the tool reads pyproject in 2026 (almost all do), use `[tool.<name>]`. Add to `check-no-legacy.sh` if needed.

antipattern.symptom: Repo configures ruff in pyproject AND keeps `.flake8` "for plugins ruff doesn't cover".
antipattern.root-cause: Author migrated 90% of config; left 10% in legacy file "just in case".
antipattern.fix: Ruff covers all flake8 plugins via `--select` codes; if a rule is missing, drop the flake8 dep entirely. Two parallel lint configs guarantee drift.

antipattern.symptom: `requirements.txt` and `requirements-dev.txt` survive migration; CI pins from txt, IDE pins from pyproject; versions drift.
antipattern.root-cause: Author treated requirements files as "the lockfile" and pyproject as documentation.
antipattern.fix: Delete requirements*.txt; let uv/pip-tools produce a single lockfile from pyproject's `[project].dependencies`.
